summary: United Against Fentanyl is a nonpartisan 501(c)(3) nonprofit fighting the fentanyl epidemic through peer grief support, national awareness events, education, and congressional advocacy. It serves families who have lost a loved one to fentanyl poisoning — primarily parents of 18-45-year-olds — along with concerned community members, offering a free network (the Fentanyl Families Network with weekly trauma-informed grief sessions), Walk for Lives events, and campaigns pressing Congress for bipartisan legislation. The organization is funded by donations and runs entirely on free, low-barrier participation.

campaigns.name: Welcome & Onboarding
campaigns.objective: Get a new newsletter or network signup to their first 'aha' moment — attending a weekly grief support session or finding a local Walk for Lives — within 7 days.
campaigns.trigger: Newsletter signup or Fentanyl Families Network membership form submitted; email 1 fires instantly.
campaigns.segment: All new contacts; branch on the form field capturing their connection to the crisis (bereaved family member vs. concerned community member).
campaigns.rationale: There is no automated welcome series today, yet the activation event — realizing you are not alone in a nationwide community — is fast, free, and self-serve. The fastest path to retention is getting a grieving newcomer into their first peer-led session before isolation pulls them away.

emails.purpose: Warm, trauma-informed welcome that honors why they came, sets expectations, and delivers one quick win: reserving a spot at this week's grief support session.
emails.subject: Welcome to United Against Fentanyl — you are not alone in this
emails.preview: Your first step: a free, trauma-informed grief session this week, led by families who understand.
emails.cta: Reserve your seat at this week's session
keyPoints: Acknowledge their loss or fear with empathy, without exploiting grief; affirm 'no other family should have to go through this'
Set expectations: free membership, weekly peer-led grief sessions, monthly crisis and policy updates — no barriers, no cost
Single first action: register for the next weekly trauma-informed session led by people with lived experience (not clinicians), so the first 'aha' lands within days
Point to a local Walk for Lives (July 11 multi-city; Sept 26 Washington DC) as an alternate entry point
Real human reply-to so a grieving newcomer can reach a person, not a no-reply address

campaigns.name: Lead-Magnet Nurture (Subscriber to Member, Donor & Advocate)
campaigns.objective: Move a passive newsletter subscriber up the engagement ladder — into network membership, a first advocacy action, and a first gift — over a multi-week cycle.
campaigns.trigger: Newsletter signup or resource-directory download with no further engagement; lead score below the 'active supporter' threshold.
campaigns.segment: Subscribers who have not yet joined the network, taken an advocacy action, or donated; branch by stated connection to the crisis.
campaigns.rationale: No nurture currently converts subscribers into members or first-time donors, and the audience's #1 objection — cynicism that advocacy produces real change — must be answered with proof before any ask lands. This sequence is the funding and advocacy engine.

emails.purpose: The proof-and-decision hero send: counter cynicism with concrete evidence that families like theirs are moving federal policy, then offer one low-friction action.
emails.subject: Your voice already moved Congress — here's the proof
emails.preview: Bereaved families are the reason fentanyl bills are advancing. Here's how to add yours.
emails.cta: Add your name — demand Congress act
keyPoints: Lead with specific, verifiable legislative momentum driven by family advocates to defeat the 'nothing will change' objection head-on
Hold strict nonpartisan framing: 'fentanyl is not a left vs. right issue — it is a human issue'
Show how individual stories compound into collective pressure, making participation feel consequential, not symbolic
Primary CTA is a low-friction advocacy action (sign / contact Congress); secondary soft path invites recurring giving that funds the advocacy work
Use a consented spokesperson family's story to make the proof human, never exploitative

campaigns.name: Booking, Reminders & No-Show Recovery
campaigns.objective: Maximize attendance at weekly grief support sessions and Walk for Lives events, and gently recover those who miss.
campaigns.trigger: Session or walk registration confirmed; reminder fires 24 hours before.
campaigns.segment: Anyone registered for an upcoming grief session or Walk for Lives event.
campaigns.rationale: Attendance is the activation event for this brand — the realization of community happens in the room, not the inbox — so a no-show is a lost 'aha.' Reminders cut no-shows by roughly 35%, directly protecting the moment that drives retention and the time-bound July 11 and Sept 26 walks.

emails.purpose: The 24-hour reminder that secures attendance at the weekly grief session, framed with zero pressure for a grieving registrant.
emails.subject: Your grief support session is tomorrow — we've saved you a seat
emails.preview: Tomorrow at 7pm ET. Come as you are — you don't need to have words ready.
emails.cta: Confirm your spot & add to calendar
keyPoints: State the exact session time and the join link clearly up top
Reassure that attending without speaking is welcome — remove the emotional barrier that drives no-shows in a grieving audience
Remind them sessions are peer-led by people with lived experience, reinforcing it is a safe, non-clinical space
Offer a one-click reschedule and an easy way to ask a question beforehand
Reuse the same structure for Walk for Lives reminders (July 11 / Sept 26) with directions and what to bring

campaigns.name: Win-Back / Re-Engagement
campaigns.objective: Reactivate lapsed subscribers and disengaged network members without guilt, and clean the list to protect deliverability.
campaigns.trigger: 60-90 days of no opens, clicks, session attendance, or event activity.
campaigns.segment: Dormant subscribers and inactive network members; branch churned-after-burnout vs. never-fully-activated.
campaigns.rationale: Emotional burnout is the explicitly named #1 churn risk — sustained grief engagement is exhausting and supporters who joined during a news spike fade when the cycle moves on. A trauma-informed 'the fight continues, and rest is okay too' hook re-opens the door without shame.

emails.purpose: Open the win-back with a no-guilt 'we understand if you needed to step back' message that honors burnout while reminding them the movement — and the door — is still here.
emails.subject: The fight didn't stop — and we understand if you needed to step back
emails.preview: No guilt. Just an open door, whenever you're ready to return.
keyPoints: Name burnout and grief fatigue directly and validate stepping away — no shame, no pressure
Remind them of the core value: a community of families who still understand their loss
Share one piece of forward progress since they went quiet to rekindle hope that change is possible
Offer a gentle re-entry: rejoin a session, or simply downshift to quiet monthly updates instead of unsubscribing
Set up the sunset path for non-responders to protect sender reputation and list health

campaigns.name: Renewal, Anniversary & Expansion (Recurring Giving)
campaigns.objective: Convert one-time donors and active members into monthly sustainers and grow giving, protecting recurring revenue.
campaigns.trigger: Donation anniversary, year-end impact moment, or a usage milestone signaling deep engagement (repeat session attendance, walk participation).
campaigns.segment: Active members and prior donors; branch high-engagement (sustainer upgrade) vs. low-engagement (re-onboard before asking).
campaigns.rationale: The organization runs on donations but has no structured recurring-giving program or upgrade journey, so committed supporters are never invited to the next rung of the ladder. A gratitude-first impact recap is the natural moment to turn episodic gifts into the sustained funding the advocacy work needs.

emails.purpose: The impact-recap hero send: quantify the collective good the supporter helped make possible, then invite them to make it last year-round as a monthly sustainer.
emails.subject: Because of you, families across the country found each other this year
emails.preview: Your impact, by the numbers — and how to keep the fight going every month.
keyPoints: Lead with gratitude and concrete numbers: families supported, grief sessions held, walkers mobilized, advocacy actions sent
Tie their specific past gift or participation to real outcomes so impact feels personal, not generic
Frame recurring giving as steady fuel for advocacy that outlasts any single news cycle — countering crisis-fade disengagement
Keep it gratitude-first and nonpartisan; honor lives lost without exploiting grief
Offer a clear, low-friction monthly amount and a human contact for questions about giving
